questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known lung cancer on palliative chemotherapy presents with new-onset severe lower back pain for 3 weeks. The pain is constant, worse at night, and wakes him from sleep. He has no leg weakness or sensory loss. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Night pain and known malignancy strongly suggest vertebral metastasis. Mechanical causes like disc herniation or fracture typically improve with rest, not worsen. Inflammatory conditions like ankylosing spondylitis improve with movement.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer on hormonal therapy has a burning, sharp pain over the right chest wall with allodynia along the mastectomy scar. The skin appears normal. She had breast surgery 2 years ago. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The neuropathic quality, allodynia, and absence of vesicular rash make post-mastectomy pain syndrome most likely. Shingles would show a rash. Local recurrence would produce a palpable mass, not just pain. Costochondritis is anterior chest wall pain with tenderness over costochondral junctions.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer (bone metastases) on regular opioids presents with a sudden, severe 'worst ever' headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness. He has no prior history of headache. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Sudden onset 'worst ever' headache with neck stiffness suggests subarachnoid haemorrhage, but in this cancer patient with known metastases, haemorrhage into a cerebral metastasis is more likely than an aneurysm. Meningeal carcinomatosis typically has a subacute progressive course. Migraine and meningitis are less likely given the abrupt onset.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with advanced ovarian cancer on oral morphine presents with colicky central abdominal pain, abdominal distension, vomiting, and absent bowel sounds. She has not opened her bowels for 2 days. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Colicky pain, distension, vomiting, and absent bowel sounds in a patient with intra-abdominal malignancy point to mechanical obstruction from tumour. Opioid-induced constipation usually lacks colicky pain and vomiting. Paralytic ileus is painless with silent abdomen. Pancreatitis and diverticulitis are less likely without appropriate triggers.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with metastatic colorectal cancer presents with burning pain and tingling in his feet and hands, starting 3 months after beginning oxaliplatin chemotherapy. Examination shows reduced pinprick in a stocking-and-glove distribution and mild bilateral foot drop. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Lumbar spinal stenosis with concurrent cervical myelopathy
questions.explanation: The temporal relationship to oxaliplatin, a known neurotoxic agent, and the length-dependent pattern strongly suggest chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy. B12 deficiency and diabetic neuropathy usually develop more gradually and are not specifically triggered by chemotherapy. CIDP is typically symmetric and proximal, not stocking-glove.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known metastatic lung cancer presents with new-onset lower back pain and bilateral leg weakness. He has no bladder or bowel symptoms. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the cause of his symptoms?
questions.explanation: Suspected spinal cord compression is an oncological emergency. MRI of the whole spine is the gold standard investigation to confirm metastatic cord compression and guide urgent radiotherapy or surgery, as per NICE guidance.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer complains of a dull ache in his lower back for 4 weeks. He is otherwise well. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the presence of bone metastases?
questions.explanation: For prostate cancer, a technetium-99m bone scan is the first-line imaging modality for detecting bone metastases due to its high sensitivity. MRI is reserved for equivocal cases or suspected cord compression.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic colon cancer presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to her back, associated with nausea. She has no fever. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the cause of her pain?
options: CT abdomen with oral and intravenous contrast
questions.explanation: CT abdomen with contrast is the best test to assess for pancreatic invasion, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, or other metastatic complications in known colon cancer. It provides detailed anatomical information.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer on long-term oral morphine for pain control presents with confusion, myoclonus, and drowsiness. Her renal function is unknown. Which investigation is most appropriate to identify the likely cause of her symptoms?
questions.explanation: Opioid toxicity, particularly from morphine-6-glucuronide accumulation, is common in renal impairment. Checking U&E (specifically creatinine) helps confirm renal failure as the cause of neurotoxicity. Other options are less specific.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with advanced ovarian cancer presents with a tense, painful abdomen and ascites. She has not had a prior paracentesis. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm malignant ascites as the cause?
questions.explanation: Cytological examination of ascitic fluid obtained via diagnostic paracentesis is the gold standard for confirming malignant ascites, as it directly demonstrates the presence of malignant cells. Imaging may suggest ascites but cannot confirm aetiology.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer reports mild back pain (numeric rating scale 3/10). He has no significant comorbidities. What is the most appropriate first-line analgesic?
questions.explanation: WHO step 1 for mild cancer pain recommends non-opioid analgesics; paracetamol is first-line due to favourable safety profile. NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) have GI/renal risks; weak opioids (codeine, tramadol) and strong opioids (morphine) are reserved for higher pain levels.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with breast cancer and liver metastases presents with moderate continuous abdominal pain (numeric rating scale 6/10). She has no renal impairment. What is the most appropriate first-line strong opioid?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends oral immediate-release morphine as first-line strong opioid for moderate-to-severe cancer pain due to ease of titration. Long-acting patches (fentanyl, buprenorphine) are not first-line; codeine is a weak opioid.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with advanced lung cancer on regular codeine 60mg QDS experiences sudden severe pain (numeric rating scale 9/10) lasting 15 minutes. He is alert and not on anticoagulants. What is the most appropriate treatment for this breakthrough pain?
questions.explanation: For breakthrough pain on weak opioids, immediate-release oral morphine is first-line. Fentanyl buccal is reserved for those already on strong opioids; subcutaneous diamorphine is for severe rapid onset; increasing background codeine is not indicated for breakthrough.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with metastatic cervical cancer has nerve root compression. She is currently on morphine SR 30mg BD but continues to report burning pain in her leg. What is the most appropriate add-on agent?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends gabapentin or pregabalin as first-line for neuropathic pain in cancer. Gabapentin is often started at 300mg TDS. Amitriptyline is an alternative but has more anticholinergic side effects; valproate and clonazepam are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with metastatic colon cancer and severe bone pain is currently on a monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI) for depression. Which analgesic is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Tramadol has serotonergic activity and with MAOIs can cause serotonin syndrome (hyperpyrexia, hypertension, convulsions). Morphine is safer but requires caution; paracetamol, ibuprofen, and fentanyl have no significant interaction.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with newly diagnosed metastatic breast cancer complains of mild pain in her left hip (pain score 3/10). She is not taking any regular analgesia. She has no renal impairment or history of peptic ulcer disease. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: For mild cancer pain, the WHO analgesic ladder recommends starting with a non-opioid. Paracetamol is first-line; NSAIDs can be considered but paracetamol has fewer GI risks. Radiotherapy is not immediate for mild pain; bisphosphonates are for prevention of skeletal events but not pain management first step.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with advanced non-small cell lung cancer has moderate pain (6/10) in his chest despite taking regular paracetamol and codeine (30mg every 6 hours). He has no significant renal impairment. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to specialist palliative care team.
Start oral morphine (immediate release) 5mg every 4 hours as needed.
questions.explanation: WHO step 2 failure (moderate pain on weak opioid) warrants stepping up to a strong opioid. Oral morphine immediate release is the preferred first-line strong opioid. Increasing codeine dose is less effective; fentanyl patch is not for acute titration; referral is not immediate.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer presents to the Emergency Department with sudden severe lower back pain (pain 10/10) and bilateral leg weakness. He is unable to pass urine. On examination, there is decreased anal tone. What is the most important immediate action?
options: Arrange urgent MRI of whole spine.
questions.explanation: Suspected malignant spinal cord compression is an oncological emergency. Guidelines (NICE) recommend immediate administration of high-dose steroids (dexamethasone) to reduce edema, even before imaging. Morphine can be given, but steroids take priority to prevent neurological deterioration. MRI is urgent but after steroid. Catheterisation deals with a symptom but not the cause. Neurosurgery might be needed but not before imaging and steroids.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with ovarian cancer is taking oral morphine sulphate modified-release 30mg twice daily for cancer pain. She has been on this dose for one week and has good pain control, but now reports constipation for 3 days despite increasing her dietary fibre. She is not on any laxatives. What is the most appropriate immediate step?
options: Switch to a different strong opioid.
Prescribe a stimulant laxative (e.g., senna) and a stool softener (e.g., docusate).
questions.explanation: Opioid-induced constipation is expected and should be managed proactively. NICE recommends laxatives (combination of stimulant and softener) for opioid-induced constipation. Reducing opioid dose would compromise pain control; switching opioid is not first-line; increasing fluids alone is insufficient; referral is unnecessary.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with advanced colorectal cancer is on long-term oral morphine sulphate modified-release 60mg twice daily. His background pain is well controlled (pain score 2/10 at rest), but he experiences breakthrough pain (7/10) once or twice a day, typically before meals. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start a fentanyl lozenge for breakthrough pain.
Prescribe oral morphine immediate release 10mg as required for breakthrough pain.
Increase the background morphine dose to 90mg twice daily.
Refer to a specialist pain team.
questions.explanation: For breakthrough pain in patients on sustained-release opioids, the recommended rescue medication is a short-acting opioid (typically the same opioid as background). Increasing background dose may help if breakthrough frequency is high, but for occasional (1-2 times/day) the first step is to provide rescue analgesia. Paracetamol is inadequate for severe breakthrough. Fentanyl lozenges are for opioid-tolerant patients but not first line. Referral is not immediate.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer presents to the Emergency Department with a 2-day history of severe mid-back pain and progressive leg weakness. He reports numbness in his feet and difficulty walking. On examination, he has bilateral lower limb weakness (power 3/5) and brisk reflexes. Which single finding most strongly indicates the need for immediate imaging and oncological review for possible spinal cord compression?
options: Sensory level at T10 on examination
Extensive vertebral metastases on recent CT scan
questions.explanation: A sensory level on examination is a classic red flag for spinal cord compression, indicating a lesion at a specific spinal level. This finding mandates urgent MRI and neurosurgical/oncological review to prevent permanent paralysis. Distractors like urinary retention (late sign) or even known metastases do not specifically mark the current emergency as a sensory level does.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with metastatic lung cancer is on regular morphine sulphate modified-release 60 mg twice daily and uses morphine sulphate immediate-release 10 mg up to four times daily for breakthrough pain. She is brought to hospital by her daughter because she has been increasingly confused for 24 hours. On examination, her respiratory rate is 8 breaths per minute, pupils are pinpoint, and she is drowsy. Which single finding confirms severe opioid toxicity requiring urgent naloxone?
options: Respiratory rate of 8 breaths per minute
questions.explanation: Respiratory depression (rate <10/min) is the hallmark of life-threatening opioid toxicity and the main criterion for administering naloxone. Pinpoint pupils are supportive but can occur without significant respiratory depression; constipation and nausea are common side effects but not emergency signs. Hypotension may occur but is less specific.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer and known bone metastases presents with a 1-week history of progressive nausea, constipation, confusion, and polyuria. She has been drinking large volumes of water. Observations are stable. Bloods are taken. Which laboratory result is most likely to confirm the life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Severe hypercalcaemia (>3.0 mmol/L) in the context of malignancy and bone metastases is a life-threatening emergency that can present with confusion, polyuria, and gastrointestinal symptoms. It requires urgent IV fluids and bisphosphonates. The other results are abnormal but not immediately life-threatening in this scenario.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with advanced ovarian cancer presents with a 2-day history of colicky abdominal pain, abdominal distension, and vomiting bile. She has not opened her bowels or passed flatus for 24 hours. Abdominal examination reveals a distended, tympanitic abdomen with high-pitched bowel sounds. A CT abdomen is performed. Which imaging finding is a red flag that requires immediate surgical or palliative intervention?
options: Complete small bowel obstruction with a clear transition point
questions.explanation: A complete small bowel obstruction with a transition point indicates a mechanical obstruction that will not resolve without intervention. This finding warrants urgent surgical or palliative consultation for decompression or stenting. In contrast, free fluid, ascites, or partial obstruction may be managed conservatively initially.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with pancreatic cancer has well-controlled pain on a fentanyl transdermal patch 75 micrograms/hour and he is also on citalopram 20 mg daily for depression. He attends the Emergency Department with a 24-hour history of agitation, hyperthermia (38.9°C), myoclonus, and diarrhoea. Which diagnosis best explains this clinical picture?
questions.explanation: The triad of altered mental status, autonomic instability (hyperthermia and diarrhoea), and neuromuscular excitement (myoclonus) in a patient on an SSRI (citalopram) and a fentanyl patch (which has mild serotonergic activity) makes serotonin syndrome the most likely diagnosis. The other options are less consistent: opioid withdrawal would have normal or high temperature but not hyperthermia and myoclonus; neuroleptic malignant syndrome usually involves rigidity and bradyreflexia; malignant hyperthermia is anaesthetic-related; neutropenic sepsis may present with fever but not typically myoclonus.
